hero.title: lintai
hero.subtitle: lintai is an offline-first security linter for AI agent configs and instructions in your repo. It checks files like SKILL.md, mcp.json, Claude settings, Cursor rules, and Copilot instructions with deterministic findings and CI-ready output.

features.title: Offline-first by default
features.description: Run it locally or in CI without sending repo instructions and agent config to a hosted scanning service.

features.title: Deterministic findings with evidence
features.description: The same repo produces the same rule ids, evidence, and exit codes, which makes review and automation easier to trust.

features.title: Targets the files that actually steer agents
features.description: The current v0.1 scope focuses on SKILL.md, mcp.json, Claude and Cursor settings, hooks, and adjacent instruction files.

features.title: Built for CI and SARIF from the start
features.description: Text, JSON, and SARIF output let teams gate pull requests without building a separate workflow just for agent checks.

features.title: Honest Stable vs Preview boundary
features.description: Stable findings are the default baseline. Preview findings are useful extra guidance, but not the main trust bar.

features.title: More than repository scans when you need it
features.description: When repo scan is not enough, scan-known, inventory-os, and policy-os help audit what local AI clients already have configured.

featuredRules.title: On-the-fly launches in hook config
featuredRules.description: Catches shared hook commands that launch tooling at runtime instead of using a reviewed executable path.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: A hook should behave like reviewed automation, not like a command path that changes under different machines or package state.
featuredRules.evidence: Committed-config signal with a concrete execution path to review.

featuredRules.title: Mutable package launcher in committed config
featuredRules.description: Catches mcp.json entries that look routine but still decide how agent tooling gets resolved and executed.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: This is the kind of change normal review underestimates because it sits in config, not in the code path it affects.
featuredRules.evidence: High-confidence signal for checked-in MCP wiring.

featuredRules.title: Broad shell grant in shared policy
featuredRules.description: Catches frontmatter and shared settings that quietly give an agent far more shell access than the workflow actually needs.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: This is one of the easiest risky defaults to miss in review because it looks like a small settings shortcut, not a power change.
featuredRules.evidence: High-blast-radius shell authority with clear remediation.

featuredRules.title: Third-party action pinned to a moving ref
featuredRules.description: Catches committed workflows that run a third-party GitHub Action by tag or branch instead of an immutable full commit SHA.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: This is one of the cleanest CI hardening signals because the risky part is concrete and reviewable: mutable third-party execution inside committed automation.
featuredRules.evidence: Low-ambiguity supply-chain control with a clear remediation path.

featuredRules.title: Copy-paste setup that becomes the team norm
featuredRules.description: Catches setup docs that normalize launching MCP tooling on the fly instead of a reviewed, reproducible command path.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: This is useful hardening guidance, but it is no longer sold as a quiet default signal because setup docs are noisier than committed executable config.
featuredRules.evidence: Useful preview signal for MCP setup drift.

featuredRules.title: Permissions that quietly overreach
featuredRules.description: Catches shared permissions like Bash(*) that turn a team default into broad shell authority.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: It is easy to copy these settings from repo to repo without noticing that the authority they grant is far wider than the task needs.
featuredRules.evidence: Straightforward shared-policy smell with obvious remediation.

featuredRules.title: Broad write access in shared config
featuredRules.description: Catches shared settings that allow wide file mutation instead of a narrow write contract tied to the repo paths a workflow actually needs.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: This kind of default is easy to inherit and hard to notice because the risky part is the breadth, not the presence of write access itself.
featuredRules.evidence: Clear write-scope issue in checked-in team policy.

featuredRules.title: Path targeting that silently fails
featuredRules.description: Catches path-specific instructions that look valid in review but do not actually target the files the team expects.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: This is a quiet failure mode: the instruction still exists, but it no longer applies where people think it does.
featuredRules.evidence: High-value contract check for shared instruction routing.

featuredRules.title: Rule routing that silently drifts
featuredRules.description: Catches shared rule config that looks internally consistent but actually sends mixed signals about where it should apply.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: Routing drift is easy to miss because the setting still looks intentional, even when the rule no longer behaves the way the team expects.
featuredRules.evidence: Clean signal for contradictory routing in shared rule packs.

featuredRules.title: Unknown key in shared rule config
featuredRules.description: Catches rule frontmatter that looks meaningful in review but is not actually part of the supported config contract.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: This creates a silent mismatch between what people think the editor honors and what the editor will really do.
featuredRules.evidence: Low-ambiguity check for quietly broken rule-pack config.

featuredRules.title: Hook command with no explicit timeout
featuredRules.description: Catches shared command hooks that leave execution bounds implicit, which makes the config harder to review and reproduce across machines.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: This is a good Claude config contract check, but it belongs in the compatibility lane rather than the headline security story.
featuredRules.evidence: Low-ambiguity config-quality signal with an easy fix.

faq.question: What is lintai?
faq.answer: lintai is an offline-first security linter for AI agent configs and instruction files in your repo. It checks the files that shape agent behavior before they become shared team defaults.

faq.question: What does lintai scan in the current v0.1 scope?
faq.answer: The current v0.1 scope focuses on files such as SKILL.md, CLAUDE.md, Cursor rules, MCP configs, Claude hook settings, local client config files, and an opt-in advisory lane for committed npm lockfiles.

faq.question: Is lintai really offline-first?
faq.answer: Yes. The core repo-scan workflow is built for local runs and CI without live network lookups during scan. Even the advisory lane uses a bundled offline snapshot by default unless you explicitly point it at another dataset.

faq.question: What is the difference between Stable and Preview findings?
faq.answer: Stable findings are the release-quality baseline. Preview findings are useful extra guidance, but they are not yet the main gating signal.

faq.question: What is the fastest way to try lintai?
faq.answer: Install the public release CLI from GitHub Releases, run lintai scan . in a repo that already contains supported files, and compare the Stable and Preview findings before deciding how hard to gate it in CI.

faq.question: Does lintai replace cloud scanners or broad supply-chain platforms?
faq.answer: No. The current v0.1 release is intentionally narrower. Think of it as a focused linter for AI agent files in your repo, not a hosted platform for every security or supply-chain problem.

download.title: Get started
download.note: Pick an install path, open the docs, and start with a fast repo-local scan.

installChannels.title: Verified shell installer
installChannels.description: Recommended Unix-like install path for the public release. The installer downloads the tagged archive plus SHA256SUMS and verifies the checksum.
installChannels.note: Best default on macOS and Linux when you want the supported release flow.

installChannels.title: PowerShell installer
installChannels.description: Supported Windows release path. Downloads the tagged archive plus SHA256SUMS and installs into a user-level bin directory.
installChannels.note: Use this on Windows when you want the same release-asset flow as the Unix installer.

installChannels.title: Direct archive + verify
installChannels.description: Manual path when you want the raw release assets and checksum verification without the convenience installer.
installChannels.note: Good when you want the release artifacts directly and will verify them yourself.

installChannels.title: GitHub Releases
installChannels.description: Browse published release notes, artifacts, and version history directly.
installChannels.note: Use this when you want the raw release surface.

installChannels.title: Docs index
installChannels.description: Open the rules, presets, and project docs published under GitHub Pages.
installChannels.note: Best next step once the CLI is installed.

quickstartSteps.title: Install the CLI
quickstartSteps.note: Use the install channel that matches your machine, then confirm the CLI is on PATH.

quickstartSteps.title: Run the first repo scan
quickstartSteps.note: Start on a repository that already contains supported AI-native files so the first run is representative.

quickstartSteps.title: Export SARIF for CI or code scanning
quickstartSteps.note: Use SARIF when you want the same scan integrated into CI and downstream tooling.

quickstartSteps.title: Inspect resolved policy when you add config
quickstartSteps.note: Useful once the target repo has a local lintai policy and you want to confirm the active preset and rule posture.

supportLanes.name: Repository-local scan surface
supportLanes.note: The current product story is the repo-local scan path for AI-native files such as skills, MCP configs, hook settings, and local client policy surfaces.

supportLanes.name: Stable vs Preview policy
supportLanes.note: Stable findings are the release-quality baseline. Preview remains useful but explicitly non-baseline and more context-sensitive.

supportLanes.name: Offline advisory lane
supportLanes.note: Dependency advisory matching is intentionally opt-in and limited to committed npm lockfiles against the active offline snapshot.

supportLanes.name: Installed artifact audit
supportLanes.note: scan-known, inventory-os, and policy-os extend lintai beyond repo scans when you need to inspect what local AI clients already have configured.
